reason: Both answers correctly frame this as existential/very worried, and both hit all four key points: diversify acquisition, capture direct user relationships (email/accounts), invest in fast-detect/fast-repair (remote config, telemetry, canary/synthetic monitoring), and treat breakage as routine rather than deepening dependence. Technical content is accurate in both (ytd-* custom elements more stable than classes, ARIA anchors, remote config vs remote code MV3 distinction, YouTube Data API as partial mitigation). Differences are minor and roughly offsetting: A adds extra nuances (conditional Web Store takedown risk, Firefox/Edge hedge, self-aware "may already be broken" caveat) plus explicit prioritization; B adds a useful SPA-specific detail (MutationObserver), graceful degradation via layered fallbacks, and a crisp "expect 2-4 breaks/year" framing. Neither has a misleading error, both are well organized, and the marginal differences cancel out.
